INDOOR_DAYLIGHT.rationale: Well-lit interior with daylight through windows. f/4 keeps most subjects sharp. IBIS helps at 1/60s.
INDOOR_DAYLIGHT.proTip: Turn off indoor lights if they mix with window light—mixed color temperatures create unflattering color casts.
INDOOR_DAYLIGHT.commonMistake: Mixing cool daylight from windows with warm tungsten room lights makes skin tones look unhealthy.

INDOOR_DIM_ROOM.rationale: Dim rooms require wide aperture and elevated ISO to maintain handheld shutter speed. IBIS critical at 1/60s.
INDOOR_DIM_ROOM.proTip: Look for pools of lamp light to use as a natural single-light portrait setup.
INDOOR_DIM_ROOM.commonMistake: Using the scene's overall light to judge exposure—always meter off the illuminated subject.

CANDLELIGHT.rationale: Candlelight is extremely dim and warm. Maximum aperture and high ISO are essential. The flickering adds to the challenge.
CANDLELIGHT.proTip: Use burst mode as candle flames flicker—some frames will be brighter than others. Choose the best exposure.
CANDLELIGHT.commonMistake: Setting WB to auto cancels the beautiful warm golden quality of candlelight—use Tungsten or 2800K custom WB.

EVENT_INDOOR_NO_FLASH.rationale: Many events prohibit flash. ISO 6400 at f/2.8 captures ambient moment while maintaining subject sharpness.
EVENT_INDOOR_NO_FLASH.proTip: Walk the venue before the event starts to identify the best-lit areas and plan your positions.
EVENT_INDOOR_NO_FLASH.commonMistake: Wandering the event without a plan results in consistently dark backgrounds and missed key moments.

NIGHT_INTERIOR_TRIPOD.rationale: Long exposures capture ambient interior atmosphere that flash destroys. Base ISO for maximum image quality.
NIGHT_INTERIOR_TRIPOD.proTip: Use HDR techniques to balance bright windows against dark interior spaces during long exposure shots.
NIGHT_INTERIOR_TRIPOD.commonMistake: Using flash ruins the authentic, atmospheric quality of available light interiors.

MUSEUM_NO_FLASH.rationale: Museums prohibit flash for good reason. f/2.8 and ISO 1600 handle most museum lighting. IBIS essential at 1/60.
MUSEUM_NO_FLASH.proTip: IBIS can let you shoot at 1/15s handheld—but artifacts must be completely still, making 1/60s plenty.
MUSEUM_NO_FLASH.commonMistake: Using a tripod that's not allowed—ask staff first or use monopod as an alternative support.

BAR_OR_RESTAURANT.rationale: Bars are typically the darkest social environments. Max aperture and high ISO essential for ambient shots.
BAR_OR_RESTAURANT.proTip: Find the areas of best light and strategically position yourself—bar counters, candles, and neon lights are all usable.
BAR_OR_RESTAURANT.commonMistake: Using flash in a bar completely changes the intimate mood and makes the background drop pitch black.

INDOOR_NATURAL_PORTRAIT.rationale: Window light creates natural, flattering window portraits. Eye detection AF ensures sharp eyes at f/2.8.
INDOOR_NATURAL_PORTRAIT.proTip: Position subject at a 45-degree angle to the window for Rembrandt lighting—the classic indoor portrait technique.
INDOOR_NATURAL_PORTRAIT.commonMistake: Positioning the subject directly facing the window creates flat, featureless light with no shadows for depth.

DANCE_FLOOR.rationale: Fast-moving dancers in dim conditions require maximum ISO and fast shutter. Colored dance floor lights add character.
DANCE_FLOOR.proTip: Sync your burst to the beat—dancers hit their peak movements on the musical beat. Anticipate it.
DANCE_FLOOR.commonMistake: Using 1/250s — fast arm movements and spinning dancers will show motion blur at that speed. Push to 1/400 and raise ISO if you still see blur.

NIGHT_STREET.rationale: Urban night shooting requires fast glass and high ISO to capture the authentic street atmosphere without flash.
NIGHT_STREET.proTip: Wet streets after rain reflect street lights, dramatically increasing ambient light and visual interest.
NIGHT_STREET.commonMistake: Not raising ISO high enough out of fear of noise—grainy night street photos have a legitimate filmic quality.

BLACK_AND_WHITE_INDOOR.rationale: High ISO noise is less visible in black and white. Mixed color temperature issues disappear in monochrome.
BLACK_AND_WHITE_INDOOR.proTip: Shoot deliberately for B&W—look for strong geometric shadows, silhouettes, and tonal contrast in the scene.
BLACK_AND_WHITE_INDOOR.commonMistake: Desaturating a color image captured for color—think in black and white from the outset.

REFLECTIONS_INDOOR.rationale: Mirrors, windows, and polished surfaces in interiors create creative layered compositions.
REFLECTIONS_INDOOR.proTip: Look for reflections in elevator doors, glass tabletops, and shop windows to create double-exposure style shots.
REFLECTIONS_INDOOR.commonMistake: Accidentally appearing in the reflection—check the entire frame before shooting near mirrors.

STAGE_PERFORMANCE.rationale: Stage lighting varies dramatically. Manual exposure prevents hunting. 1/250 freezes most performance movement.
STAGE_PERFORMANCE.proTip: Identify the best-lit moments during rehearsal or sound check to know where to position for the actual performance.
STAGE_PERFORMANCE.commonMistake: Shooting during the entire performance rather than identifying and targeting the peak emotional moments.

INDOOR_SPORTS.rationale: Indoor court sports need 1/500 to freeze fast movement. ISO 6400 is a necessary trade-off for sharpness.
INDOOR_SPORTS.proTip: Use the highest frame rate your camera offers (20fps+) to maximize chances of catching the perfect peak action.
INDOOR_SPORTS.commonMistake: Using a slow telephoto when you need a fast telephoto—can't make up for insufficient aperture at high ISO.

FIREPLACE_LIT_SCENE.rationale: Firelight is warm, directional, and constantly flickering. Burst mode captures frames at varied brightness levels.
FIREPLACE_LIT_SCENE.proTip: Set WB to Cloudy (6500K) to preserve the warm orange glow of firelight without making it look grey.
FIREPLACE_LIT_SCENE.commonMistake: Auto white balance neutralizes the beautiful warm fire glow—set a custom warm white balance.
